2606.27103.title: The Riddle Riddle: Testing Flexible Reasoning in Large Language Models and Humans
2606.27103.paper_summary: Humans flexibly adapt their reasoning strategies to the requirements of a given problem. Large language models (LLMs) have performed well on many cognitive tasks, however, it is unclear whether this accuracy is a result of pattern matching from training data or flexible reasoning. Here, we introduce a novel paradigm to test this question: the riddle riddle paradigm. Riddle riddles are word problems written to mimic popular riddles, but altered so their answers only require literal interpretations. Identifying correct answers requires looking past the structure of each question and flexibly apply different reasoning strategies based on the content. If LLMs respond to surface features, such as form, a riddle-like structure should cause models to use an inventive reasoning strategy even when a literal interpretation suffices. Alternatively, if LLMs reason based on content, they should flexibly switch strategies when appropriate. Across two experiments with nine state-of-the-art LLMs and 100 human participants, we show humans and LLMs fail on this paradigm in opposite directions. LLMs were far more accurate on genuine riddles than on riddle riddles (84.9% vs. 50.7%); whereas humans showed the reverse effect (50.5% vs. 80.5%). Error analysis shows that 90.8% of LLM errors on riddle riddles (the condition where they show diminished performance) were due to inappropriate use of inventive reasoning while only 57.6% of human errors on genuine riddles were due to overextending literal reasoning. Thus, while both groups make mistakes, reasoning mistakes are made more often by LLMs than by humans. Overall, LLMs' strong performance on genuine riddles may reflect memory retrieval rather than flexible strategy selection, and without stimuli designed to elicit this contrast, it becomes easy to conflate LLM-generated outputs that look like reasoning with genuine reasoning.
2606.27103.paper_summary_zh: 人類靈活地調整他們的推理策略以適應特定問題的需求。大型語言模型（LLMs）在許多認知任務上表現良好，然而，目前尚不清楚這種準確性是訓練數據的模式匹配結果還是靈活推理的結果。在這裡，我們引入了一種新的範式來測試這個問題：謎語謎語範式。謎語謎語是為了模仿流行謎語而編寫的文字問題，但經過修改，使其答案僅需字面解釋。識別正確答案需要超越每個問題的結構，並根據內容靈活地應用不同的推理策略。如果LLMs回應表面特徵，例如形式，則謎語般的結構應該使模型即使在字面解釋足夠的情況下也使用創造性的推理策略。另一方面，如果LLMs基於內容進行推理，則在適當的情況下應該靈活地切換策略。在對九個最先進的LLMs和100名人類參與者進行的兩個實驗中，我們顯示人類和LLMs在這一範式上的失敗方向相反。LLMs在真正的謎語上的準確性遠高於在謎語謎語上的準確性（84.9%對50.7%）；而人類則顯示出相反的效果（50.5%對80.5%）。錯誤分析顯示，90.8%的LLM在謎語謎語上的錯誤（即它們表現不佳的情況）是由於不當使用創造性推理，而只有57.6%的人的錯誤在真正的謎語上是由於過度延伸字面推理。因此，雖然兩組都會犯錯，但LLMs的推理錯誤比人類更為頻繁。總體而言，LLMs在真正謎語上的強勁表現可能反映了記憶檢索而非靈活的策略選擇，而在沒有設計來引發這種對比的刺激下，將看似推理的LLM生成輸出與真正的推理混淆變得容易。
2606.27103.author: Bella Fascendini et.al.
2606.27103.authors: Bella Fascendini, Kathryn McGregor, Max D. Gupta, Thomas L. Griffiths
